paragraphs.text: Joseph commanded his brothers to say before Pharaoh: “Thy servants are shepherds, both we, and also our fathers” (Gen 47:3), so that Pharaoh might settle them in the land of Goshen, and through this they might be separated from those who worshipped sheep and calves. And Pharaoh said to Joseph: “In the best of the land make thy father and brethren to dwell… And Joseph brought in… his father… unto Pharaoh; and Jacob blessed Pharaoh… and… went out from before him” (Gen 47:6–7, 10).

At first Joseph sold grain to the Egyptians for silver. Then, when the silver was used up, he sold for cattle. And finally, in order to feed them, he sold even for fields, “except the land of the priests only, for he bought that not” (Gen 47:22); because the priests received free the bread appointed to them by Pharaoh. In the seventh year of famine Joseph gave the Egyptians seed, “and made it a law… that they should give the fifth part to Pharaoh” (Gen 47:26).

“And the days drew nigh that Jacob must die… and he said… to Joseph: Put thy hand under my thigh” (Gen 47:29). As Abraham adjured Eliezer by the covenant of circumcision, so Joseph swore to Jacob that he would bury him “with his fathers,” and Jacob “bowed himself… upon the top of his staff” (Gen 47:30–31).
